title_tesim: 在校內血液的處理
alternative_title_tesim: Blood handling in schools
Zai xiao nei xue ye de chu li
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Blood precautions in schools: To safeguard oneself against contracting blood-borne diseases including AIDS and Hepatitis B, appropriate precautionary measures should be adopted and applied generally. 1. Equip the first aid box with essential items including disposable gloves, cotton wool, gauze and antiseptic for emergency use. 2. Don’t touch blood or wounds with bare hands. 3. Wear disposable gloves when handling wounds or blood-stained materials. 4. Dress all wounds properly after cleaning. 5. If direct contact with blood or body fluids has occurred, wash promptly and thoroughly with soap and water. 6. Clean all blood-stained surfaces with household bleach. 7. Blood-soiled gloves, towels, cotton wool, dressing etc. should be put in plastic bag and sealed for disposal. 8. Handle sharp objects carefully to avoid injury, e.g. 捳椀猀猀漀爀猀Ⰰ 瀀椀渀猀Ⰰ 渀攀攀搀氀攀猀Ⰰ 最氀愀猀猀 椀渀猀琀爀甀洀攀渀琀猀⸀ 㤀⸀ 䄀瘀漀椀搀 琀栀攀 甀猀攀 漀昀 栀甀洀愀渀 戀氀漀漀搀 漀爀 挀攀氀氀 猀愀洀瀀氀攀猀 椀渀 氀愀戀漀爀愀琀漀爀礀 攀砀瀀攀爀椀洀攀渀琀猀⸀ ㄀　⸀ 匀攀攀欀 洀攀搀椀挀愀氀 愀搀瘀椀挀攀 椀渀 挀愀猀攀 漀昀 搀漀甀戀琀⸀ 倀甀瀀椀氀猀 猀栀漀甀氀搀 戀攀 愀搀瘀椀猀攀搀 琀漀 椀渀昀漀爀洀 琀栀攀 琀攀愀挀栀攀爀猀 椀昀 愀渀礀 愀挀挀椀搀攀渀琀猀 漀挀挀甀爀 愀渀搀 栀愀瘀攀 爀攀猀甀氀琀攀搀 椀渀 戀氀攀攀搀椀渀最⸀㰀⼀椀渀猀挀爀椀瀀琀椀漀渀㸀

Another man is standing behind him, leaning down, and pinching his nipples. In the background, leaning on the wall of a building, is another couple making out. Next to the couple in the foreground is another handcuffed wrist, but the image is cut off by the edge of the poster. The text of the poster suggests that how one prefers to have sex is not the problem. AIDS is transmitted by not using appropriate protection.

Poster depicts two young men standing in front of a fountain-like structure. They are gazing intently at each other, while the man on the left is in the process of pulling at the unbuckled pants of the other young man, whose underwear is exposed. The background is classic, beautiful and romanesque, but is slightly out of focus. The camera is clearly accentuating the actions of the young men, and is suggestive of the sexual activity that is about to take place. The poster text suggests that feelings of lust are natural and beautiful, and that the use of protection should be considered to be a natural part of sex.

title_tesim: Si Da. No Da. No cambies tu vida por el SIDA [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Yes this transmits. No this doesn't transmit. Don't change your life because of AIDS
publisher_tesim: Spain. Ministerio de Sanidad y Consumo
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item SP002.

Additional poster text. Si Da: Intercambiarse la maquinilla de afeitar y otros objetos personales cortantes. Transmisión de la madre al feto. Tatuaje con agujas sin esterilizar. Hacer el amor con portadores (Hombre-mujer). Relaciones homosexuales con portadores. Utilizar la misma jeringuilla. Intercambiarse el cepillo de dientes. No Da: Practicar cualquier tipo de relaciones sexuales utilizando perservativo. Bañarse con portadores. (Duchas, piscinas, etc.). Las picaduras de insectos. Jugar con portadores. Beber del mismo vaso o la misma botella. Darse la mano. Besarse and abrazarse. Cambiarse la ropa. Utilizar los mismos servicios que los portadores.

Images of female and male gender symbols engaging in different activities. On one side of the poster, they are depicting and describing activities that can increase the risk of transmission of the HIV virus. Activities such as having unprotected sex with a person infected with the virus, sharing needles, toothbrushes or razors. One the other half, the symbols are depicting and describing activities through which transmission is not possible or the risk of transmission is decreased. Some of these activities include having protected sex, kissing, hugging and shaking hands.

"Párate un momento, escucha y aprende todo lo que puedas acerca del VIH/SIDA. La prevención y la vida... ¡están en tus manos!" Joey DiPaolo, educador sobre el SIDA que vive con VIH.

description_tesim: Top half of the poster is textual, with subheadings "How do you catch the AIDS virus?" "Why do we need to protect ourselves?" and "Let's talk about AIDS." title_tesim: How AIDS is spread
caption_tesim: Uganda School Health Kit on AIDS Control
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: AIDS is spread mainly through sexual intercourse with an infected person. AIDS is spread through blood contaminated instruments. AIDS is spread through infected woman during pregnancy to her unborn child. AIDS is spread through infected blood transfusion. Prevent AIDS! Have sexual intercourse with a faithful partner for life. Accept medical treatment only from trained health workers in health centres where equipment is kept sterile and blood is checked against AIDS.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster describes four ways by which AIDS is spread: sexual intercourse, contaminated medical instruments, pregnancy and blood transfusions. Illustrations depict a naked man and woman in silhouette, a man receiving medical treatment from a traditional healer, a kneeling pregnant woman, and a woman receiving a blood transfusion.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item UG001.

title_tesim: What does a person with AIDS look like? AIDS can look like many other diseases. Don't be confused. Don't spread rumors

En découvrant l'histoire vraie d'un enfant de leur âge, les écoliers prennent consciences d'autres réalités et d'autres modes de vie. Meteor se compose d'un film, d'une brochure et d'un dossier pédagogique.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Date on back of poster, 2006, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item BE011.

Translated additional poster text: Health, everyone's right! Meteor is a tool for fostering humane values, aimed at children 10 to 12 years old. By hearing stories from children their age, students become aware of other realities and other ways of life. Meteor consists of a film, a booklet, and teaching manual.

Poster depicts the face of a girl, Valery, in one large square panel bordered on its left edge by three smaller square panels showing Valery in different scenes: the top panel shows her with classmates; the middle one shows her holding a flip board presentation on AIDS and STD's; and the bottom one shows her being embraced by a younger girl. A fourth circular panel in the upper right corner of the main panel shows an urban landscape, most likely Valery's hometown. The poster serves as an advertisement for Meteor, an educational kit that teaches children about other children affected by AIDS.

description_tesim: A colorful graphic illustration of people, with a background suggesting clouds and snowy mountains. One man is playing a fiddle while another looks on, and there are music notes next to them. Another man (wearing an AIDS ribbon) is adjusting the neckerchief of a man in traditional native American dress. There is a mother (perhaps an Inuit woman, suggested by her clothing) with a child on her back. Finally, there are a man and woman holding hands. The graphic illustrates the textual message that there is hope for more supportive communities and hope that together, HIV/AIDS can be overcome.
At the bottom of the poster, there is a red AIDS ribbon and the text December 1, Nunakakaakhimaut AIDSNI Naluhuiktailiktakhait Ubla [Tiisiivva 1, Nunaqaqkaaqsimajut Aukut Tusaumanirmut Ullunggat].

description_tesim: Against a background of rolling green hills and a river, native Canadians are depicted interacting with each other. In the foreground, an older woman is shown cooking for a group surrounding her. To her left is a young man wearing graduation robes and holding a feather. Behind them, two women are talking; one is holding a clipboard and wearing an AIDS ribbon. To the right, a young boy is being handed a bowl by a kneeling older man. The poster suggests that native people can and should take a leadership role in prevention the spread of HIV/AIDS and providing support for those already infected.

description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR269.

Poster depicts a naked woman from the waist up, with her arms crossed over her chest (partially hiding her breasts). Her posture suggests hopelessness. Poster also has information on the Hortefeux law in France which affects immigrants.

Translated poster text: The project of the Hortefeux immigration law further restricts the family by creating particularly discriminatory conditions. Foreign women suffering from serious diseases like AIDS have only one alternative: to be doomed to remain alone in France to heal or die with their relatives in their country of origin, lacking of available treatments.

condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: 1er prix concours de dessins: Robertson Dale, 1er prix concours de slogan "Tauo noa" Apolina Teapiki (1/12/92). Robertson Dale, 23 ans (Bora Bora) allie le traditionnel au moderne. Le couple trés typè avec des tatouages, dans des vêtements traditionnels, révèle une posture respectueuse d'écoutre à l'autre. Au centre, une boule assimilable à une alliance ou à un préservatif, irradie des rayons d'énergie. Tout s'éclaire avec le message écrit. Un clin d'oeil est donné à l'ensemble par une frise de couples de "tikis" protecteurs et porters de préservatifs.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FP001.

Poster depicts a drawing of a couple in traditional attire kneeling and facing each other, holding hands. In the space between their torsos, there is an object that looks like an eye, or possibly a condom, radiating beams of light or energy toward the couple. A black and white border depicts two repeating drawings of Tiki couples. In one, the Tiki couple stands arm in arm; in the other, the couple faces each other, and the male Tiki wears a condom. Poster suggests that couples that respect each other will practice safe sex, and that the Tikis play a protective role by promoting condom use.
Part of the "Taua noa" campaign.

repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Frontal nude of muscular suntanned man, from nose to thigh, showing a penis of much lighter skin shade than the rest of the body.  He has protected his penis from the summer sun, AIDS and sexually transmitted diseases, presumably by using condoms. In the lower right-hand corner is the logo of the AIDES association, with "AID" in upper-case white letters, the "e" in lower-case red, and the "S" in white upper-case. To the immediate left is a graphic of the red AIDS ribbon.
Date on back of poster, 2002, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR003.

description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item NE026.

Nkosi Johnson, a South African child born HIV-positive and who later developed AIDS and died at the age of 12, is shown speaking on a wireless microphone to an audience at the 13th International Conference on AIDS in Durban, South Africa. Poster's message is that it is "Better Late Than Never" to understand the dangers of AIDS. Poster implies the existence of AIDS as long as there are unprotected activities. Picture in the lower left corner is part of the AIDS Fonds banner and the picture in the lower right corner represents AIDS Memorial Day.

description_tesim: The poster lists 10 suggestions for those who might be administering an HIV test or counseling about HIV testing. It reminds people to be sensitive, polite, careful, clear, patient, etc., when dealing with individuals who might be seeking or undergoing HIV testing. The poster has a blue border, and its right side is adorned with colorful question marks. An outline of a man is tumbling down the question marks, suggestive of a person who has many questions about HIV and might be feeling helpless.
Date on back of poster, 1997, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item IT001.

title_tesim: VIH/SIDA: os factos [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: HIV/AIDS: the facts
publisher_tesim: Portugal. Commissão Nacional de Luta Contra a SIDA
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: A SIDA não tem cura... Não discrimine!
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster presents the facts about the transmission of HIV and AIDS. Divided into two parts, poster presents a sequence of images to separate the facts from the misperceptions. AIDS is transmitted through: unprotected sex, contact with contaminated blood, from mother-to-child during pregnancy and breast-feeding, and sharing of needles. AIDS is not transmitted through: food or silverware, mosquito bites, coughing and sneezing, shaking hands and hugging, use of public restrooms, safe sex (condom), and social contact.
Date on back of poster, 2002, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item PO004.

One would be the symbol of death caused by AIDS and also the sign of courtship which is shown by the snake's tongue, it's sensual organ. Poster says to live life the way one desires, however keep your eyes open for awareness. Eye of a animal in the lower right corner symbolizes wildness and a heightened awareness.

description_tesim: Two muscular young men, clad only in undershorts and shorts, are enjoying a picnic in a grain field.  In the foreground, one man lies on his back on a blanket, shorts open in front and pulled down to reveal his undershorts. He holds hands and looks fondly into the face of his lover, who is sitting up next to him, dressed only in undershorts.  Their eyes display love for each other.  An open picnic basket sits nearby, and one can see some bread, some wine, some watermelon, and some red condoms. The scene is one of idyllic pleasure, and also of safety and security.
Part of "Stop AIDS" campaign.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item SZ020.

title_tesim: Ohne? Ohne mich
alternative_title_tesim: Without it? Without me
Stop AIDS
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Eine Präventionskampagne der Aids-Hilfe Schweiz in Zusammenarbeit mit dem Bundesamtes für Gesundheitswesen.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item SZ018.

A color photograph shows a man dressed in a suit and tie seated behind a desk in an office, surrounded by shelves full of books. His closed right hand is raised with thumb pointing up in a "thumbs up" sign; on  his thumb is a condom. The word "ohne" (without) is next to the condom. The man is probably in his thirties, wears glasses, and has a professional desk job, perhaps as an archivist, lawyer or accountant. The message is that a mature, well-educated professional gay man will not have sex unless condoms are used for protection.

title_tesim: Escludere per l'AIDS? Escluso!

repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item SZ009.

Shows a nude man floating in front of a triptych of stars, a moon, and a cloud with an angel. Below him is an assortment of items associated with gay culture (a book of Robert Mapplethorpe's photography, a Freddie Mercury album, a Keith Haring painting) as well as holiday decor and wrapped presents. The poster may represent the gifts of gay culture to the mainstream and argue against thinking of the gay community as separate and the only part of society that has to deal with AIDS. It also suggests the decimation of the gay community by AIDS, as represented by the three artists, each of whom died of AIDS.

His left arm rests on the top of the door, while his right arm is toward the viewer; his right hand is closed in a "thumbs up" sign, with a condom on his thumb. He wears a black leather jacket and his sunglasses are shoved back on the top of his black curley hair.  The message is that a sporty young gay man will not have sex unless condoms are used.

A color photograph shows a man and a woman playfully relaxing in a small boat, secluded by some reeds at the edge of a large lake. The man, dressed in undershorts, is leaning back on a bench in the boat, looking at the woman and teasing her by dangling her bra from one outstretched hand.  The woman is facing him, seated on another bench, and is splashing him with a little water from the lake. She is covered by some clothing draped from breast to hips. Next to the man is a straw bag draped with an article of clothing; on top are seen two red unopened condoms.  The scene is one of sexy playfulness, love, fun, safety and security because the man uses condoms for protection.

Si tu resultado es positivo, trabaja junto con un médico para tomar las decisiones que serán las mehores para tí. Cuanto más rápido lo hagas, más le ayudará la atención médica. Consulta con un médico, con su departamento de salud o los otros centros locales para el SIDA. Llama al teléfono especial para el SIDA de su estado o zona local, o al teléfono nacional al 1-800-344-7432. Para las personas con impedimentos del oído, llama al 1-800-243-7889 (TTY). HIV es el virus que causa el SIDA.

The poster depicts a drawing of a blue, old-fashioned dial telephone, on a blue background which incorporates the words, "solo sesso sicuro." Into the design of the phone dial are integrated the signs for male and female; the holes in the dial show the numbers for the hotline telephone number.  The theme of this poster is that safe sex prevents the risk of AIDS, and that more information about AIDS as well as free, anonymous HIV testing are available by calling Gruppo C's hotline number.

Austrian soccer star Helge Payer is shown bare-chested facing the viewer. He plays as a goal keeper, suggesting the importance of behaving defensively to keep the AIDS virus out of your body. He is looking straight at the onlookers and his hands are open in such a way that he is defending his goal.  There is a glowing light behind him, and his hands seem to be holding an invisible soccer ball. Two condoms, still in their packages, are shown on the bottom right of the poster; one has an inscription ASK FOR, askö wien. The inscription is followed by a website www.askoe-wien.at (a sports related website). The other condom has the logo of AIDS HILFE which is a drawing of a outline of a house with the AIDS ribbon on it. The words AIDS HILFE appear on the bottom of the logo.

title_tesim: Passive fyre har altid HIV – eller hvad? [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Passive guys always have HIV -- or what?
condition_note_ssi: Text in bottom left corner of poster: Design: Mikkel Sonne; Foto: Morten Wøldike; Tryk: Eks-Skolens Treykkeri APS.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Date on back of poster, 2006, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.

Translated additional poster text: Dangerous conjectures? Most men use condoms and lubrication, when they have sex with a new partner. Every now and then some men use other methods to avoid being infected or infecting their sex partner. Some try to guess whether their sex partner is HIV-positive or not. It can be worth it to look at his age, what he does in bed, or how one met. But neither age, meeting place or sexual experience can tell whether a person is HIV-positive or not.

A male with his back turned is pulling down his jeans to reveal his buttocks. A drawing of a heart with an arrow coming out of the top right (modeled on the male symbol?) appears in the top right corner as a logo for the STOP AIDS organization. Poster questions whether gay men who play a more submissive role (i.e. "bottoms") in sexual situations are more likely to be HIV-positive, and encourages viewers to avoid making assumptions.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item DK014.

title_tesim: Grande simulatrice [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Grand illusionist
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Les IST* ne sont pas toujours visibles. Faites un dépistage.*Infections sexuellement transmissible
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster depicts an unmade bed. Two pillows are partially shown: one is black and the other is black-and-white, with a pattern of circles encompassing radially-oriented swirls, reminiscent of the motion that induces hypnosis. A similarly patterned comforter is pulled back to reveal a white satin sheet with thin folds arranged in the shape of the word: Syphilis. The phrase "La Grande Simulatrice" is inscribed underneath in a red dot-matrix, suggestive of bullet holes.  Possibly encourages the user to have the stealth of a skilled hunter to track down and eliminate undetectable STDS--by practicing safe sex.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item BE012.
Translated additional poster text: Sexually transmitted diseases are not always visible. Track them down.
Date on back of poster, 2006, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.

title_tesim: Test HIV [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Stop AIDS
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Fare il test e'importante per te e per gli altri. Stop AIDS, Progetto del Ministero della Sanità, ex Secedas. In collaborazione con Gruppo C Network Italiano.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Underneath the title, "Test HIV," is a large black arrow pointing downwards to words which translated reads, "Getting the test is important for you and for others."Along the left side of the poster in white lettering on a blue background is a textual explanation of how AIDS is transmitted (sharing needles, from sexual relations, from an infected mother to her baby). Also explained is the need to take a simple blood test, and advice about how it is voluntary and anonymous to do so; the results are confidential. Finally there is the warning that many people have contact with HIV without knowing it.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item IT012.

condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: There are many different STIs-and some you would not know you have without a check up. Using condoms protects against many common STIs as well as unwanted pregnancies and HIV. Leaving an STIs untreated can turn a simple problem into a serious one, and may leave you more vulnerable to HIV infection. Have a check up, for the sake of your sexual health.

repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: This poster is a collection of colored drawings in a comic book style. These pictures tell a story of a gay man who is looking for love. It resembles a situation comedy, for instance, the hero is watching TV and getting information on different subjects. The story is building up to a conviction that he could have unsafe sex, because in the news he is hearing that scientists have some drugs that improve the immune systems of people with AIDS. When he meets with his partner he ridicules him for trying to use condom; he declares "haven’t you seen the news on TV?" When he learns that his partner does not have TV, he tells him you are so uniformed, how do you get through life?
Date on back of poster, 2005, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR088.

publisher_tesim: France. $b Ministère de la santé publique et de l'assurance maladie
condition_note_ssi: Translated poster text: This summer, it will be perhaps the first time. The inexperience, emotion, and a certain apprehension may be disconcerting during your first intercourse. There is no need to have dozens of experiments to test or to experience pleasure ; hugs, kisses, discovering the body of another, are all sources. The condom does not necessarily make things easy, but it's important to talk about it before and use it right the first time and every time thereafter. This is the most effective way to protect yourselves from the AIDS virus (HIV) and sexually transmitted diseases (STDs). It is also a means of contraception. But mostly, it's  a proof of love. To speak to someone about it, call Youth Health at 05.235.236. AIDS Info Service and 05.36.66.36. Protect yourself from AIDS.

condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Le monde a promis de stopper le SIDA d'ici 2015. Que ferez-vous?L'objectif du Millénaire relatif au SIDA vise à stopper Ia propagation du SIDA et à commencer à inverser Ia tendance d'ici 2015. Pour atteindre cet objectif, des promesses ont été faites et des engagements ont été pris au niveau individuel, communautaire et national. Engagez-vous dans Ia Campagne Mondiale contre le SIDA pour vous assurer que ces promesses sont tenues. Pour plus de détails, rendez-vous sur www.worldaidscampaign.org

Translated additional poster text: The world has promised to stop AIDS by 2015. What will you do?The Millennium Development Goal on AIDS is to halt the spread of AIDS and begin to reverse the trend by 2015. To achieve this goal, promises were made and commitments were made at individual, community and national levels. Engage in the Global Campaign against AIDS to ensure that these promises are kept. For details, visit www.worldaidscampaign.org

Pour arrêter l'épidémie, il est essentiel d'éliminer les inégalités et injustices sociales et économiques qui nourissent sa progression. Responsabiliser les individus et les communautés pour faire face au sida est un élément critique et fondamental du respect, de la promotion et de la valorisation de leurs droits humains. Stop SIDA. title_tesim: HIV/AIDS. Fears. Discrimination. It can end with us [inscribed].
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: There is an additional inscription next to the four pictures of native Canadians:The Canadian Aboriginal AIDS Network Inc. (CAAN) is a non-profit coalition of individuals and organizations which provides leadership, support, and advocacy for Aboriginal people affected and infected by HIV/AIDS regardless of where they reside. These four Aboriginal people in the photo to the left have all been affected by HIV/AIDS, fear and discrimination in some way, and come forward as a part of this year’s Aboriginal AIDS Awareness Day Poster Campaign to tell their stories. It is the goal of these individuals, and of all the members and associate members of the Canadian Aboriginal AIDS Network, to make sure that all Aboriginal people in Canada have the facts about HIV, both to protect ourselves and our families, and to help those of us already infected with the disease achieve the quality of life that we deserve. For more information on Aboriginal people and HIV/AIDS call the Canadian Aboriginal AIDS Network toll-free at 1-800-285-2226 or go to our webpage at WWW.caan.ca.

The cover of the document Permis de Séduire [License to Seduce] is also displayed. Its design imitates a Permis de Conduire [driver's license], and it contains a condom and instructions on how to use it, as well as information on the modes of transmission of HIV.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item BE004.

title_tesim: Mine de rien, on trouve des lieux de prévention du sida à tous les coins de rue [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Protégez-vous du sida. Protégez les autres
You may not have noticed, but there are AIDS prevention locations on every street corner
caption_tesim: Votre pharmacien, acteur de la prévention du sida
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: En un an les pharmacies ont delivré 52 millions de préservatifs, 1.8 million de Stéribox (R) et plus de 10 millions de seringues, ce qui les place en première ligne de la tutte contre le sida. Aujourd'hui les pharmaciens mettent à la disposition des usagers de drogues des seringues vendues à l'unité. C'est important, car une seringue ne doit servir qu'une fois et ne doit pas être partagée. Le pharmacien sait aussi accueillir, écouter, conseiller et orienter: il est proche de vous.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences

Text appears over a photograph of a Paris street. On the left, sticking out from a building, is a lighted "plus" sign, representing a pharmacy.  The poster stresses the importance of pharmacies as places to obtain condoms and new syringes. Pharmacists are available to listen and offer advice and support.
Date on back of poster, 2000, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.

title_tesim: Heu-reux: Anne et Philippe se réveillent tranquillement [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Protégez-vous du sida

condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Cette nuit, ils ont fait l'amour en se protégeant. Ils se servent d'un préservatif lubrifié. Ils ajoutent un gel à base d'eau en cas de séecheresse vaginale pour ne pas gêner Anne et pour toute pénétration anale, afin d'évite tout risque de rupture du préservatif. Ces gels à base d'eau sont en vente dans les pharmacies et certaines grandes surfaces. Pour en parler, Sida Info Service au 05.36.66.36.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Date on back of poster, 1997, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR017.

The top portion of the poster is a photograph of a man and a woman, standing on a balcony, with a house opposite in the background.  They hold bowls of steaming breakfast food. One sees the woman's face looking affectionately at the man, but his face cannot be seen. They are casually dressed, having just awakened from a night of protected sex.  The text promotes use of condoms coated with water-based gel for comfort and for protection of the condom against breakage. A colored picture of a condom is inset at the bottom.

title_tesim: Bien assurer la première fois, c'est simple comme lire une B.D. [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Protégez-vous du sida
To be well-insured the first time is as simple as reading a comic strip
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Cet été, ce sera peut-être la première fois. L'inexpérience, l'émotion, la hâte, la peur de ne pas assurer peuvent décontenancer lors d'un premier rapport sexuel.  Mais pour assurer, on n'est pas obligé de battre des records. En prenant son temps, en parlant ensemble, en essayant le préservatif tout seul avant, on est plus rassuré...
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Date on back of poster, 1997, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR019.

To the left of the text is a four-image panel, arranged vertically, with drawings illustrating the steps in using a condom: place it on the end of the erect penis, smooth it all the way down, and remove it when finished.  The message is directed to young people who are sexually inexperienced, and seeks to reassure them that using a condom every time provides good protection. The comic-strip style of the illustrations emphasizes how easy it is to learn to use a condom and adopt this as a habit. A colored picture of a condom is inset at the bottom.

title_tesim: Belgian AIDS poster presenting the means by which the virus is not transmitted in the workplace [descriptive]
condition_note_ssi: Poster text: A condition de ne pas melanger le sexe et le travail, il n'y a aucun risque de transmission du virus du sida dans l'entreprise.  [If you do not mix sex with work, there is no risk of transmission of the AIDS virus in the workplace]
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Objects commonly found in the workplace are pictured with captions explaining that they are not means for transmission of the AIDS virus: Coffee mug, ashtray, bar of soap, postage stamps, telephone receiver, scissors, awl, screwdriver, pencil, toilet seat, compass, razor knife, first aid kit, bottled water, french fries, a fork in a piece of pie, a pair of partially-full wine glasses, and a hand shake.  Also depicted is a condom in a foil packet.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item BE008.

description_tesim: Illustration of a pink condom, as seen when fully filled out. Underneath it appear the words, "forme anatomique" (anatomically correct). description_tesim: Poster presents a sequence of illustrations which show how HIV cannot be transmitted: man shaking hands; man laying on a couch with his cat and swatting a fly; man on a ladder in a pool wearing a swim cap with a fish next to him; man covered in soap suds standing next to a hanger and washing machine; man exiting a public restroom; man holding a hammer jumping to reach a nail; two children playing with blocks; man drinking from a public water fountain; man running with flowers in his hand for a hospital visit; man sitting at a table with a plate in front of him holding a large knife and fork; man and woman hugging and kissing; man surrounded by two large telephone receivers; doctor examining a patient; man donating blood.

A colored picture of a condom is inset at the bottom.

repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Depicts the heads and torsos of two young men, one leaning back to look into the eyes of the other positioned slightly behind him. Both are smiling. Each word of the poster text is placed on top of a pastel-colored oval, arranged in angled rows over the bottom third of the poster. The words suggest places and activities which appeal to both young men and women. These same places, emotions, and activities could be associated with sexual encounters and relationships between both gay and straight couples.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item GW147.

title_tesim: Hans schläft sicher bei Otto. Felix schläft ruhig zu Hause. Sein Hans benutzt Kondome

repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Date on back of poster, 2004, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item GW145.

Depicts a naked man, laying face down, on a billowy bed. The poster text indicates that this is Felix, who is home sleeping, while his lover Hans is with another man named Otto. Felix has no reason to fear, because Hans uses protection when he has sex with other partners.

title_tesim: SIDA: hoy X hoy, la única, vacuna: información y prevención [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Day X Day, the only, vaccination: information and prevention
publisher_tesim: Madrid (Spain : Region). Servicio Regional de Salud
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Asi no se contrae el SIDA [with depictions of how AIDS can't be contracted underneath]; Asi se previene el SIDA [with depictions of how to prevent it].
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster advocates arming oneself with knowledge about preventive measures as the most effect "vaccine" against AIDS. It depicts some of the misperceptions about AIDS transmission: you can't get it by kissing, holding hands, playing with an AIDS patient, using the same utensils as an AIDS patient, or using the same toilet. It also depicts ways to prevent contracting AIDS: don't use the same razor or toothbrush, use a condom, and use unused syringes.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item SP009.

title_tesim: Women, girls and HIV/AIDS [inscribed]
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Women and Girls are three to six time more likely than men to contract HIV.  Take the lead NOW.  Trust in Protection.Having sex with only one partner can be high-risk behavior -- because your partner's sexual behaviour.  Trust in Protection. Marriage or living with someone does not guarantee protection against HIV infection.  Take the lead NOW.  Trust in Protection.Women and girls are twice as likely as men to contract HIV from a single act of unprotected sex, yet they leave protection up to men.  Take the lead NOW.  Trust in Protection.Men, boys and social institutions can help improve the plight of women and girls when it comes to the AIDS epidemic.  Support protection for women.Education and economic empowerment can stop the spread of HIV.  Not chance.  Not luck. Trust in protection.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item TR001.
Date on back of poster, 2005, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.

The poster is a long narrow poster with a 2005 calander printed on it. At the top of the poster there are five photographs of smiling faces of people of different races and ages. Under the pictures there is a red AIDS ribbon with several country flags on one end of the ribbon and the logo for Caribbean Epidemiology Centre (CAREC) PAHO/WHO on the other end of the ribbon. At the bottom of the poster there are logos for the three main organizations that have published and sponsored the poster.  The websites for the Caribbean Epidemiology Centre are: www.carec.org and www.catin.org.

title_tesim: What am I? [inscribed]
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: I’m free ‘n’ easy and fun to pick up. I’m easily passed on. I can be invisible and damage your health. I’m a sexually transmitted disease. Most STIs are easily treated, but if left can cause serious health problems. Regular check-ups are recommended to detect STIs which may go unnoticed.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Background stripes of pink, green, purple, and blue; text in white & black. Depicts four rows of curios. Row 1: drawing of Freud's profile with naked man as face; toothbrush with popsicle on stick; pineapple filled with ice; bear sculpture with banana as tail. Row 2: "Ken" doll; ceramic teapot shaped as cottage; photo of gnome and pink flamingo lawn decorations; greeting card with picture of cowboy. Row 3: hand grenade; pen with photo of nude man; pastille candies with red hearts. Row 4: Bandages of the invisible man; green & blue bugs; mer-man.

Sponsors: Community HIV and AIDS Prevention Strategy; Gay Men’s Project; Slap fM; Gay Men Fighting AIDS; Healthy Gay Manchester; Gay Men's Health Matters; BIGUP; Yorkshire MESMAC; Naz Project London; The Network.

title_tesim: Their son was diagnosed HIV+. Their son went to them for support. Their son was told he wasn't their son anymore. [inscribed]
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Then, their son came to us. He told us his story. We listened. We explained that his parents weren't evil. Just ignorant. Overwhelmed by fear. That's why London Lighthouse provides information, care and support for anyone affected by HIV and AIDS. In fact, his parents are now talking to us. Which is a start. Straight or gay. Old or young. White or black. Him or her. AIDS doesn't care. We do. We need you to as well.

title_tesim: When Martin was told he had AIDS, his family couldn't believe it. After all, he is 64 [inscribed]

Him or her. White or black. Straight or gay. AIDS doesn't care. We do. We need you to as well.

title_tesim: Nós preocupamo-nos....e você?  [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: We worry about ourselves...and you?

Campanha Mundial Contra a SIDA 2001
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: "Disse alguém que as mulheres carregam metade do céu. Se assim é, as mulheres seropositivas carregam o céu inteiro. Como mulher, sinto a obrigação de contribuir para a mudança da situação de milhões de mulheres que vivem com VIH/SIDA. Porque são sujeitas a uma dupla discriminação, mostram uma coragem do tamanho do universo."  Maria José Campos, médica, vice-presidente da Comissão Executiva Coordenadora da Rede Mediterrânica de Mulheres Confrontadas com a infecção peolo VIH. ["Someone says that women carry half of the sky, if that's the case then seropositive women carry the whole sky.  As a woman I feel the obligation to contribute to change in the situation of millions of women that live with HIV/AIDS.  Because they are subject to double discrimination, they demonstrate courage the size of the universe." Maria José Campos, doctor, vice-president of the executive commission, coordinator of the Mediterranean Network of Women Confronted by the HIV infection.]

condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Farlige formodninger? De fleste mænd bruger kondom og glidecreme, når de har sex med en ny partner. Af og til bruger nogle mænd dog andre metoder til at undgå at blive smittet eller at smitte sex-partneren. Nogle prover at gætte sig frem til, om sex-partneren er hiv-smittet eller ej. Det kan være ved at se på hans alder, hvad han gor i sengen, eller hvor man mødte hinanden. Men hverken alder, mødestad eller seksuel erfaring kan fortælle, om en person er smittet med hiv eller ej.

Translated additional poster text: Dangerous conjectures? Most men use condoms and lubrication, when they have sex with a new partner. Every now and then some men use other methods to avoid being infected or infecting their sex partner. Some try to guess whether their sex partner is HIV-positive or not. It can be worth it to look at his age, what he does in bed, or how one met. But neither age, meeting place or sexual experience can tell whether a person is HIV-positive or not.
Organization web site: http://www.farligeformodninger.dk

A man with a pierced ear and a tattoo on one arm, naked except for a white towel around his waist. His face is shiny and his hair is damp, as though he has just come out of a sauna. A drawing of a heart with an arrow coming out of the top right (modeled on the male symbol?) appears in the top right corner as a logo for the STOP AIDS organization. Poster questions whether gay men who frequent bathhouses are more likely to be HIV-positive, and encourages viewers to avoid making assumptions.

description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item IT013.

The text on this poster appears on an illustration that resembles a mirror-image Rorschach ink-blot design, with the top half mirrored on the bottom (which is upside-down).  The design suggests a group of people, and is colored with a watercolor wash of orange, green and brown shades. The top group of people is divided from the upside-down bottom group, illustrating the message that if fear divides us, AIDS will grow. At the bottom of the poster appears a logo: two telephone receivers with a red drop of blood in between them.

title_tesim: Not until you're diagnosed HIV positive do you discover how sick people can get. [inscribed]
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Hate mail. Abusive phone calls. Even aggressive confrontations and physical attacks. This is how some people who are HIV positive are treated. And not just by strangers. By work colleagues, by former friends and sometimes even by close family. And it's not unheard of either for people with HIV to be discriminated against by health workers. Yet the fact is, there's no reason for them to be treated differently from anyone else. You can't contract HIV by working or socialising with someone who has it. And thanks to new treatments, its possible for people with HIV to lead full and active lives. All that's stopping them is other people's ignorance. Are you HIV prejudiced? To learn the facts, go to www.areyouhivprejudiced.org. By ending ignorance, we'll end prejudice.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences

Translated poster text:Sex without a condom when you are HIV-positive, is endangering his health.The over-contamination from another HIV strain is for you a real risk, as is the co-infection with certain STDs which are transmitted more easily between sero-positives.Use condoms during each sexual intercourse whether your partner is HIV negative or seropositif. Doing so minimizes all your risks.

title_tesim: C'est Pierrot, il a eu chaud [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: SIDA, 12 affiches pour en parler
This is Pierrot, he was hot
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster number 7 in series: " Le SIDA, 12 affiches pour en parler."
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR287.

Poster tells a story in pictures. On the first one the man Pierrot is sitting in the bar with his friends. On the second picture he is visiting a sick man, who is lying in the bed with infusion. On the next one Pierrot is sitting in the doctor’s office with worry expression on his face. Then the nurse is taking his blood to do the test. The calendar on the wall shows that the date is May 3rd. On the next picture Pierrot is visiting doctor again possibly to find out what the blood test result. The date on the calendar is May 18th. The doctor gives him some papers to read and possibly telling him to be careful. On the next picture Pierrot possibly buying condoms from the vending-machine. Then the nurse is taking his blood for blood test again. The calendar in the nurse’s office shows that the date is August 3rd, which means that 3 month have passed by. On the next picture Pierrot is sitting in the doctor’s office. The date on the calendar is August 18th. The doctor is smiling, which possibly means that Pierrot doesn’t have anything serious. On the last picture Pierrot is going with the little box of medication and singing. Poster is drawn black and white.

description_tesim: Poster number 6 in series: " Le SIDA, 12 affiches pour en parler."
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR286.

Poster divided on four horizontal sections. First section includes three parts: Picture of a sick person lying in bed with infusion, picture of infusion apparatus with a dot in it, which represents some kind of infection, and image of a drop, which probably also represents infection. Second section consists of a picture of two men and a woman using drugs, picture of syringe with a dot in it, which represents some kind of infection, and picture of a drop. Third section depicts pregnant woman sitting on a bench, picture of fetus inside of a female body with a dot on placenta, which represents infection, and image of a drop. Last section includes picture of naked man and woman that hug each other, image of a male and female genitalia with secretions with a dot, which represents some kind of infection, and picture of a drop. Poster is drawn black and white.

description_tesim: Poster tells a story in pictures. On the first picture young woman is looking on the baby clothes in the store window. On the second picture the same woman is sitting in the gynecologist’s office, the woman is pregnant. Then the nurse is taking woman’s blood for testing. On the next picture the women is sitting in the office of another doctor and crying. Possibly the doctor had told her the bad news that she is HIV positive. On the next picture the woman is sitting with her husband on a couch at home, and her husband is trying to calm her down and show her his support by touching her shoulder. On the next picture nurse is taking blood from the husband’s hand for testing. Then he is buying condoms from the vending-machine. On the next picture the same young woman feeding a baby with milk from a bottle. On the last picture two nurses are possibly taking the blood from the baby to test, or giving the baby a treatment in vein. Poster is drawn black and white.

title_tesim: Triste historie de M. Rouletabille [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: SIDA, 12 affiches pour en parler
Sad story of Mr. Rouletabille
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster number 8 in series: " Le SIDA, 12 affiches pour en parler."
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR288.

Poster tells a story in pictures. On the first picture two men are sitting in the restaurant and drinking alcohol. The waitress is bringing cake. The name of one of them is Mr. Rouletabille. There are trucks can be seen in the window of the restaurant, so the men possibly are truck drivers. On the second picture Mr. Rouletabille sitting with his wife and child in the living room and watching TV. The program that they are watching is about AIDS. The big letters “AIDS” are on the screen. On the next picture Mr. Rouletabille is sitting in the doctor’s office and talking to the doctor. Then nurse is taking his blood for the test. On the next picture Mr. Rouletabille is sitting in the doctor’s office again and crying. Possibly doctor had told him the bad news, that he has AIDS. Then doctor makes physical exam of Mr. Rouletabille. On the next picture the nurse is taking the blood for test from Mrs. Rouletabille. Then Mr. Rouletabille possibly is buying condoms from the vending-machine in the store. His child is standing nearby and drinking soda. On the next picture Mr. Rouletabille is working, driving the truck. Then Mr. Rouletabille is sitting in the wheelchair and the nurse is taking him to the doctor. After that Mr. Rouletabille is driving the truck again, but now he looks very sick. On the last picture Mr. Rouletabille is lying in the bed in the hospital with infusion. His wife is standing near him and handing his hand. Poster is drawn black and white.

description_tesim: Poster shows an example of the artwork of Edith Hirsch and advertises an exhibit and sale of her art held in 1988 to benefit Österreichische AIDS-Hilfe. The painting is of several images superimposed: a butterfly, a woman's open legs, and an unrolled condom. In the lower right corner is a small green inchworm. The World AIDS Day logo for 1988 appears at the bottom of the poster, and apparently it fell during the dates of the exhibit.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item AU046.

condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Bien utilisé, le préservatif, associé à un gel exclusivement à base d'eau, est un moyen efficace de se protéger du virus du sida (VIH) et des maladies sexuellement transmissibles (MST) lors d'une pénétration anale entre hommes. Les gels à base d'eau sont en vente dans les pharmacies, certains grandes surfaces et sex-shops. Voici quelques conseils pratiques pour une efficacité optimale de ces produits.

title_tesim: Protection maximale [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Maximum protection
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Bien utilisé, le préservatif est, lors des rapports sexuels, la meilleure protection contre le virus du sida (VIH) et les maladies sexuellement transmissibles (MST). Vérifiez que la marque NF figure sur l'emballage: cela signifie que les préservatifs ont été soumis aux contrôles de qualité de la norme française, l'une des plus strictes d'Europe. Une notice d'utilisation est délivrée dans chaque boîte. Consultez-la. Enfin, en cas de sécheresse vaginale et pour toute pénétration anale, pour éviter tout risque de rupture du préservatif, utilisez également un gel à base d'eau, en vente dans les pharmacies et dans certaines grandes surfaces. Pour en parler, Sida Info Service au 05.36.66.36. Protégez-vous du sida.

title_tesim: Who will make war against AIDS? [inscribed]

description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR064.

Poster depicts the Statue of Liberty holding a machine gun and a bomb. With the black background, title in red letters and the use of an iconic symbol suggesting war, poster promotes political activism in the fight against AIDS and implies that the U.S. is less aggressive in the war against AIDS than in other situations that result in military intervention.

Depicts a door handle covered by a condom. Poster addresses a misperception about the transmission of the AIDS virus. Poster suggests through the image (suggestive representation of a penis) and text (penetrating) the actual mode of transmission of the AIDS virus through sex, and the importance of using a condom as protection.

title_tesim: Nicht jede Verbindung ist eine feste

Gib AIDS keine Chance
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Depicts a young woman on the telephone, one hand caressing the phone cord. She is wearing a cocktail dress and has a string of pearls around her neck. The poster text "eine Feste Verbindung" is being used in reference to a clear telephone connection, which relates directly to the poster's graphic. The phrase can also be interpreted as "a permanent connection," as in a permanent relationship with another person. The poster is then suggesting that not all relationships are free of "static" or are permanent, indicating that no one should be taking chances with a sexual partner. Protection should always be used.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item GW126.

description_tesim: A safety pin is shown pinned onto the lapel of a man's suit. The poster image is a black and white photo showing the left-hand side of a man's torso, from his chin to his shoulder; he is wearing a tie, a shirt with button-down collar, and a suit. Vertically along the right side of the poster are five olive-yellow boxes containing the texts, with a little safety pin image next to each one.  What the safety pin stands for are: new feelings, responsibility, solidarity, trust, and open verbal communication. If a man practices safe sex, his wearing of the safety pin would let others know that they can talk with him about it.

The photo evokes a sombre mood of lonliness and fear, which encourages to viewer to think about being in the same state as the young man, and emphasizes the need to stop AIDS.

title_tesim: She told her husband she was HIV+. He took it badly. She told her best friends. They took off [inscribed]
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: She told us. We listened. And listened. She told us she'd spent her life caring for others and that now, when she really needed hope, there was no-one. That's why London Lighthouse provides information, care and support for anyone affected by HIV and AIDS. By the way, she left her husband and is now rebuilding her life again. Her or him. Young or old. Gay or straight. Black or white. AIDS doesn't care. We do. We need you to as well.

description_tesim: Date on back of poster, 1996, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.

title_tesim: Derek lost his entire family to AIDS. One year later, they're still not talking to him. [inscribed]
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: That's why he called us. We always listen. We never judge. We know that family rejection can sometimes be more distressing than the illness. That's why London Lighthouse provides information, care and support for anyone affected by HIV and AIDS. We're still trying to help Derek's family understand. Black or white. Him or her. Young or old. Straight or gay, AIDS doesn't care. We do. We need you to as well.

title_tesim: Amy's dad couldn't bring himself to accept he had AIDS. Now he can. He just can't bring himself to tell her. [inscribed]

That's how we work. That's why London Lighthouse provides information, care and support for anyone affected by HIV and AIDS. Amy doesn't understand yet, but one day, she will. Young or old. Him or her. Black or white. Straight or gay. AIDS doesn't care. We do. We need you to as well.

title_tesim: Huellas peligrosas [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Dangerous tracks
America responde al SIDA
condition_note_ssi: Ana está saliendo con un chico que se inyecta drogas. Ella está cometiendo un error grave. El puede adquirir el SIDA por compartir las agujas de las jeringas. Ana podría adquirir el SIDA por culpa de él. Ella tiene varias opciones: Hacer que él deje de inyectase drogas o buscarle ayuda profesional y tratamiento contra las drogas; u olvidarse de él, o por lo menos asegurarse que cuando tienen relaciones sexuales, él use un condón.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item US012.
Depicts the arms of a couple holding hands. His arm shows needle marks from intravenous drug use. Poster suggests a relationship with risks: intravenous drug use, unprotected sex.
Part of "America responde al SIDA" campaign.

Five arms reach upwards; the highest-reaching hand holds a condom. Another arm reaches down with hand open, ready to take the condom. At the bottom, right-hand corner: a small image of a red ribbon over the image of a group of islands [Seychelles?] surrounded by a thin boarder shaped like what appears to be a splitting cell. The text: SIDA, it's everybody's problem, sourrounds the whole image.

A black and white photograph of a family group with a young black woman and her two elementary school-age children, a girl and a boy. title_tesim: If you cheat on your partner you could wind up with more than just a broken heart.
condition_note_ssi: Poster text: If you cheat on your partner you could wind up with more than just a broken heart. With AIDS around, cheating on your partner could cheat you both out of your lives. AIDS is deadly. Even one encounter could infect you with the AIDS virus. So what started out as a harmless flirtation could turn into a deadly moment of truth. That's why it's important to know your partner. Because a broken heart is bad, but not as bad as a broken life. For more information about AIDS, call 1-800-342-AIDS. America responds to AIDS

title_tesim: What have you got against a condom? [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: America responds to AIDS
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: What have you got against a condom? The simple act of putting on a condom can save your life, if they're used properly and every time you have sex. For more information about AIDS and condoms, call 1-800-342-AIDS.

title_tesim: Hey! You know what? You can't get AIDS from...
condition_note_ssi: Hey! You know what? You can't get AIDS from: eating at restaurants, using toilets in public spaces, mosquito bites, giving someone a hug or kiss, sharing food with friends, playing on playgrounds, visiting someone who is sick with AIDS, going to the doctor, AIDS is not spread by things we touch!
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences

title_tesim: Some people think they come of age when they've been burnt by gonorrhea...
alternative_title_tesim: America responds to AIDS
condition_note_ssi: Some people think they come of age when they've been burnt by gonorrhea, but if they've been burnt by AIDS they may never come of age. To find out more about the relationship between AIDS and VD, call the National AIDS Hotline at 1-800-342-AIDS. The hotline for the hearing impaired is 1-800-AIDS-TTY. America Responds to AIDS
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences

title_tesim: Don't just worry about HIV. Do something about it.
alternative_title_tesim: America responds to AIDS
condition_note_ssi: Poster text: Don't just worry about HIV. Do something about it. If you think you are at risk for HIV infection, now's the time to consider counseling and testing. If you test positive, work with a doctor to make decisions that are right for you. The earlier this happens, the more medical care can help. Talk to a doctor, your health department, or other local AIDS resources. Call your State or local AIDS hotline, or the National AIDS Hotline at 1-800-342-AIDS. Call 1-800-243-7889 (TTY) for deaf access. HIV is the virus that causes AIDS. America responds to AIDS.

title_tesim: Nós preocupamo-nos....e você?  [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: We worry about ourselves...and you?
Porque a SIDA existe!
Campanha Mundial Contra a SIDA 2001
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: "A informação objetiva, isenta, não discriminatória e sem avaliações moralistas é um dos vectores essenciais da Prevenção, associada à disponibilidade e fácil acesso aos meios de prevenção e redução de riscos e a um ambiente social favorável." "Por isso nós informamos, editando, desde 1996, a única publicação periódica em português de e para as pessoas com VIH/SIDA."  Pedro Silvério Marques Seropositivo, sócio fundador, membro da Comissão Executiva e director do Boletim da Abraço. ["Objective information, exempts without discrimination and without moralist evaluations as one of the essential factors of prevention associated with the availability and easy access to means of prevention and risk reduction and to a socially favorable environment.  That's why we inform by publishing, since 1996 as the only periodical information in portuguese for people with HIV/AIDS."  Pedro Silvério Marques Seropositive, partner founder, member of the Executive Commission and director of Boletim da Abraço.]

title_tesim: Aktive fyre har aldrig HIV – eller hvad? [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Active guys never have HIV -- or what?
condition_note_ssi: Additional poster text: Farlige formodninger? De fleste mænd bruger kondom og glidecreme, når de har sex med en ny partner. Af og til bruger nogle mænd dog andre metoder til at undgå at blive smittet eller at smitte sex-partneren. Nogle prover at gætte sig frem til, om sex-partneren er hiv-smittet eller ej. Det kan være ved at se på hans alder, hvad han gor i sengen, eller hvor man mødte hinanden. Men hverken alder, mødestad eller seksuel erfaring kan fortælle, om en person er smittet med hiv eller ej.
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Date on back of poster, 2006, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item DK013.

Translated additional poster text: Dangerous conjectures? Most men use condoms and lubrication, when they have sex with a new partner. Every now and then some men use other methods to avoid being infected or infecting their sex partner. Some try to guess whether their sex partner is HIV-positive or not. It can be worth it to look at his age, what he does in bed, or how one met. But neither age, meeting place or sexual experience can tell whether a person is HIV-positive or not.
Organization web site: http://www.farligeformodninger.dk

A young man in a green t-shirt and black jeans stands with one hand hitched in his pants pocket. A drawing of a heart with an arrow coming out of the top right (modeled on the male symbol?) appears in the top right corner as a logo for the STOP AIDS organization. Poster questions whether gay men who are dominant (i.e. "tops") in sexual situations are less likely to be HIV-positive, and encourages viewers to avoid making assumptions.

Based on the San Francisco "Assumptions" campaign, the slogan reads, "Comment fais-tu pour savoir?" English version is: "How do you know what you know?" www.penses-y.ca

A poster with two black-and-white photos placed side-by side, showing the upper bodies of two men, one behind the other, both facing to the left of the scene.  The man on the left is light complected, with short dark hair, mustache and beard, and his eyes are closed.  The second man is posed behind the first; he has dark skin and his eyes are also closed. The positions suggest that they have just had anal intercourse. Each man is making an assumption about the other and whether each is HIV-positive or negative. Poster text suggests the importance of being informed about HIV to prevent contracting it.
Poster is copyright 2004 Cabra Diseño and San Francisco AIDS Foundation. www.cabradiseno.com

description_tesim: Other people involved in poster creation: Raúl Cabra, Andy Williams, Wesley Ito, Liz Craig and Jillian Moffett.

This poster depicts a grainy black and white photograph of a young white man with his head and chest visible, lying face-up on his back with his head on a pillow.  His eyes are shut and his brow is furrowed, lips are slightly open, suggesting that he is in the act of intercourse.  The text of the poster suggests the importance of not making assumptions, and of knowing about the other person’s HIV status to prevent contracting it.
Based on the San Francisco "Assumptions" campaign, the slogan reads, "Comment fais-tu pour savoir?" English version is: "How do you know what you know?" www.penses-y.ca

Picture of two young adults, brother and sister leaning one on another and smiling. Poster in tints of bluish red. Poster suggests the importance of speaking up against homophobia and discrimination based on sexuality.

title_tesim: On meurt encore du SIDA à Paris. Protégez-vous [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: We are still dying of AIDS in Paris. Protect yourself.
condition_note_ssi: At bottom of poster: Sida info service 0 800 840 800, appel anonyme et gratuit
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster depicts a map of "Les Halles-Beaubourg" section of Paris. Three locations on the map are marked with a black dot surrounded by the text: "Vous n'êtes plus içi" [You are no longer here]; these dots and text mimic the standard map marking "Vous êtes içi" [You are here]. The dots symbolize deaths from AIDS.

Part of an anti-AIDS campaign from the Mairie of Paris. The logo for the campaign appears in the lower left corner of the poster: the words "Paris Plaisirs, Paris Capotes" [Paris pleasures, Paris rubbers] circling an image of a condom.
Poster issued in 2003 (Base documentaire, Banque de donées, santé publique)
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR132.

title_tesim: Tour de France
alternative_title_tesim: The tour around France
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Date on back of poster, 2005, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.

Comic strip of two men lying on the beach and reading the diary of a man they had each slept with separately in the past. They discover the numerous sexual partners he has had. The men realize that he lied to them when he told them they were his first sexual partners. Taking him at his word, they didn't use a condom at the time, but are now regretful and fearful of having exposed themselves to HIV. The title is a play on the bicycle tournament in France of the same name.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR098.

title_tesim: Lieux du crime [incribed]
alternative_title_tesim: Crime spots
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Comic strip of a gay couple walking through a backroom. Due to his belief that backrooms are breeding grounds for AIDS transmission, one of the men urges his partner to leave. The same man later dismisses the use of a condom at his own place, rationalizing that as long as they aren't in a backroom, they are safe from AIDS.
Date on back of poster, 2005, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR099.

title_tesim: We talk [inscribed]
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Date on back of poster, 2004, reflects when the vendor received the copy later acquired by the UCLA Library.
In white border at bottom of poster:  "For more information."  A logo in red for GlaxoSmithKline Positive Action at Work. And a second logo for NAT with a red ribbon National AIDS Trust.
Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item UK053.

Additional poster text: --We talked about everything. I hoped that meant we'd never have to talk about whether one of us might have HIV. It's taken time, but we're talking again. That's a start. But I wish we had talked about it before.

title_tesim: Sida ne fait pas que des morts. Il fait aussi des orphelins. [inscribed]
alternative_title_tesim: AIDS does not just kill. It also orphans.
condition_note_ssi: Translated poster text: In 2001, over 13 milion children have lost one or both parents to AIDS ... in 2010, it will be 26 million. Why is it important to take action? These children are exposed daily to malnutrition, deprivation of their property, lack of education, alcoholism, crime ... Left to themselves, they are the target of numerous acts of violence: economic exploitation of their labor, abuse ... Orphans are becoming the main victims of sexual assault and in some countries they are the first enlisted in armed conflict. AIDS orphans are a particularly fragile generation due to being exposed to an early HIV infection. Help us help them.To sponsor an orphan or send donations, contact AIDS Orphans International
repository_tesim: Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History and Special Collections for the Sciences
description_tesim: Poster held by Louise M. Darling Biomedical Library. History & Special Collections, Collection no. 306, item FR163.

description_tesim: The Wawona Tree was 27 feet in diameter and 227 feet tall. A tunnel was cut through the tree in 1881, enlarging an existing fire scar. The tree had a slight lean, which increased when the tunnel was completed.  The tree fell in 1969 under an estimated two-ton load of snow on its crown.

Horse-drawn buggy driving through a tunnel in the Wawona Tree in Mariposa grove in Yosemite National Park. The wagon is seen from the back. Three people are in the wagon and two people stand on either side of the wagon.

The mission was founded by Father Presidente Fermin de Lasuén on December 8, 1787, as the 11th of 21 Franciscan Missions in California, near the small settlement of Lompoc. This building contained the padre's home, office, and chapel.

There are many pedestrians and 2 dogs visible. A horse is pulling 2 men in a buggy on the left. Legible signs include, from left to right, "Western Hotel Restaurant Grill Room Open Day and night", "Money transferred by  telegraph & cable office," "The Call," "Paystation," "S. G. King Co. Dry Goods," "Carpets and Linoleum," and "Powell Bros. Props. The Racket. Handles nearly everything".

description_tesim: Small wooden crosses mark the graves in the cemetery bounded by a picket fence. One grave is marked with a larger wooden cross. A small area within the cemetery is bounded by another picket fence. A few houses are visible in the distance.

Santa Ysabel Asistencia, established in 1818 as a sub-station to Mission San Diego de Alcala, was located east of San Diego in the area north of the town of Santa Ysabel. The cemetery is all that remains of the original site, which included a chapel, granary, and several adobe houses.
Same image as no. CHS-736 in the USC, Title Insurance and Trust / C.C. Pierce Photography Collection

title_tesim: San Antonio de Pala Asistencia, view towards cemetery, bell cote and chapel, Pala, circa 1900

description_tesim: General view of the cemetery, bell cote (bell gable, campanario, espadaña) and chapel of San Antonio de Pala Asistencia. The Pala Mission was a sub-mission to Mission San Luis Rey de Francia, some twenty miles inland upstream from the mission on the San Luis Rey River. The cemetery, behind an eroding adobe wall, contains the remains of hundreds of Indian converts and early California settlers and was still in use as of 2005. Mountains are visible in the background.

description_tesim: The bell cote (bell gable, campanario, espadaña) at San Antonio de Pala Asistencia, which contains two bells, is unique among the California missions as a free-standing structure. The original bell cote was destroyed by torrential rains in 1916 and was replaced by a precise replica immediately. The Pala Mission was a sub-mission to Mission San Luis Rey de Francia, some twenty miles inland upstream from the mission on the San Luis Rey River. Mountains are visible in the background.

title_tesim: View towards the town of Avenal in the Salinas valley with the Santa Lucia mountain range in the distance, Avenal, circa 1929
alternative_title_tesim: C.C. Pierce & Co., 1572 W. Pico St., L.A., 19
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: C. C. Pierce Photographic Collection,UCLA Library Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library.
description_tesim: View down a dirt road towards the town of Avenal which appears as a narrow band of buildings and power poles along the main road through town, with the Santa Lucia mountain range in the background. The buildings appear to be mostly houses, a few businesses and a church on the far right. There is also an oil rig on the center-right side of town. Legible signage in the photograph includes: Golden Rule Shoe Shop, The Republic Supply Co. of California, and H. C. Smith MFG Co.

The identification of the town as Avenal was made on the basis of the visible signage in the photograph and the presence of the oil rig. In 1928 the Milham Exploration Company struck oil, turning this agricultural community into a boomtown. The Republic Supply Company is listed in Wikipedia as one of the first business established at Avenal at this time. In 1929, the California-based H. C. Smith Manufacturing Company was sold by its founder, Herman C. Smith, to Global Oil Tools.

title_tesim: Hugo Reid Adobe and old mission bell at Rancho Santa Anita, Arcadia, circa 1900-1903
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: C. C. Pierce Photographic Collection,UCLA Library Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library.
description_tesim: At the time the photograph was taken, the adoabe was the residence of Elias J. ("Lucky") Baldwin. Originally a ranch of the Mission San Gabriel Archangel, founded in 1771, the Rancho Santa Anita had many owners after the secularization of the the missions in 1833. The photograph shows a view of a walkway bordered by Eucalyptus trees that were planted by owner William Wolfskill in 1865. To the left a wall of the 1839 adobe house of first post 1833 owner, Hugo Reid, can be seen, with the wood framed wing added by owner Elias Jackson Baldwin in 1879 visible in the background. Also visible is a "mission" bell hanging from a small, wooden structure at the end of the walkway.

title_tesim: Original adobe chapel at the San Francisco de Asís Mission, San Francisco, circa 1887
alternative_title_tesim: Mission Dolores, San Francisco
named_subject_tesim: San Francisco de Asís Mission (San Francisco, Calif.)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library Special Collections
funding_note_tesim: Access to this collection is generously supported by Arcadia funds.
dlcs_collection_name_ssm: C. C. Pierce Photographic Collection,UCLA Library Special Collections, Charles E. Young Research Library.
description_tesim: Original adobe chapel at the Mission Dolores, with a graveyard visible at its side. The larger basilica is barely visible at the edge of the photograph. La Misión San Francisco de Asís was founded June 29, 1776, by Father Francisco Palóu, under the direction of Father Junipero Serra. Its original name was La Misión de Nuestro Padre San Francisco de Asís (The Mission of Our Father Saint Francis de Assisi). It was commonly known as Mission Dolores, after a nearby creek called Arroyo de Nuestra Señora de los Dolores (Our Lady of Sorrows creek). It is the oldest original intact Mission in California and the oldest building in San Francisco.
combined_subject_ssim: San Francisco de Asís Mission (San Francisco, Calif.)
